name: Joshua
description: Joshua, son of Nun, was Moses' successor and a pivotal figure in leading the Israelites into the Promised Land after the Exodus from Egypt. Originally named Hoshea, Moses renamed him Joshua, meaning "God is Salvation". He served as Moses' aide and was one of the twelve spies sent to Canaan. Unlike the other spies who spread fear, Joshua, along with Caleb, brought back a positive report, demonstrating faith in God's promise. After Moses' death, Joshua was divinely appointed to lead the Israelites, crossing the Jordan River miraculously and conquering Canaanite cities like Jericho and Ai. His leadership was characterized by unwavering obedience to God's commands, military prowess, and the fair distribution of land among the twelve tribes. Joshua's story is a testament to faith, courage, and the fulfillment of divine promises, solidifying his place as a central figure in Jewish history and a symbol of leadership and devotion. He oversaw the renewal of the covenant at Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim, and his final address to the Israelites emphasized the importance of remaining faithful to God. His legacy is one of faithfulness, obedience, and the realization of God's promises to the Jewish people.
subtitle: Moses' successor, conqueror of Canaan
achievements: Led Israelites into the Promised Land

keyMyths.title: Crossing the Jordan River
keyMyths.description: God miraculously stopped the flow of the Jordan River, allowing the Israelites to cross on dry ground, symbolizing a new beginning. This event mirrors the parting of the Red Sea. The priests carrying the Ark of the Covenant led the way.

keyMyths.title: The Fall of Jericho
keyMyths.description: Jericho fell after the Israelites marched around the city for seven days, with priests blowing trumpets, and the people shouting. The walls of the city miraculously collapsed. Rahab and her family were spared.

keyMyths.title: The Sun Standing Still
keyMyths.description: During a battle against the Amorites, Joshua prayed to God, and the sun stood still in the sky, allowing the Israelites to achieve victory. This miracle demonstrated God's support for Joshua. This provided ample time to defeat the enemy.
